title: Cytosine arabinoside induced gastrointestinal toxic alterations in sequential chemotherapeutic protocols.A clinical-pathologic study of 33 patients
abstract:
CYTOSINE ARABINOSIDE INDUCED GASTROINTESTINAL 
TOXIC ALTERATIONS IN SEQUENTIAL 
CHEMOTHERAPEUTIC PROTOCOLS 
, A  Clinical-Pathologic Study of 33 Patients 
RICHARD E. SLAVIN, MD,*$ MARCO A. DIAS, MD,§ AND REIN SARAL, MDt 
Sequential chemotherapeutic regimens, primarily used in the treatment of 
hematopoietic malignancies, and employing ara-C as a basic antineoplastic 
agent induce mucosal alterations in the entire gastrointestinal tract. These 
are characterized by surface and glandular epithelial atypia, immaturity, and 
necrosis. Glandular regeneration is characteristically delayed leading to a state 
of intestinal aproliferative cytopenia. Other toxic intestinal changes include 
telangiectasia of blood vessels and the formation of intramural hematomas. 
Intestinal infections develop frequently and are complicated by peritonitis, 
liver abscesses, pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis and sepsis. These intestinal 
lesions are accompanied by a predictable clinical syndrome which begins con- 
comitantly with ara-C infusions and is characterized by diarrhea, ileus, 
abdominal pain, hematemesis and melena, severe hypokalemia, hypocalcemia 
and a protein-losing enteropathy. Additional toxic manifestations induced by 
ara-C include transient weight gains, fever elevations and severe bone marrow 
depression. The genesis of the intestinal lesions is linked to the three day 
dose schedule of ara-C infusions which insures both arrest of the cycling 
intestinal cells in the S-phase and a high cytotoxic index. The severity of 
these lesions is markedly augmented by prior treatment with ara-C and 
cyclophosphamide which causes synchronization and probable recruitment of 
intestinal stem cells, respectively. 
Cancer 42:1747 -1759, 1978.

complications occurring in the gastrointesti- 
nal tract in autopsied patients treated with 
sequential chemotherapeutic protocols utiliz- 
ing cytosine arabinoside (ara-C) as a basic 
antineoplastic agent. 
MATERIALS 
AND METHODS 
Patient Selection and Therapeutic Modality 
Five protocols considered in this study with 
pertinent clinical information, dosage of treat- 
ment with each drug, sequential spacing, as 
well as survival time after initiation of each 
therapeutic combination are indicated in 
Table 1. The patients were assigned to the 
listed protocols after thorough clinical evalua- 
tion and histopathologic typing by means of 
bone marrow or tumor biopsy. All patients 
were treated per primum except for those 
with chronic myelogenous leukemia in blast 
crises, malignant lymphomas and lymphosar- 
coma cell leukemia. Three patients on the 
CAO protocol received 60 mg/mz of predni- 
sone a day for 21 days and 0.025 mg/kg of 
vincristine once a week for three weeks im- 
mediately prior to the initiation of this regi-

men. None of our patients were given prior 
x-irradiation. Only patients receiving ara-C as 
part of their protocol were included in this 
study. Chemotherapeutic agents were ad- 
ministered in the following manner. The 
ara-C was given parenterally as a continuous 
infusion for 72 hours either as a single course 
or in two courses in all patients except for 
those patients expiring before course comple- 
tion. One patient received 3 separate courses 
of ara-C and in one patient there was a proto- 
col violation. Daunorubicin was given as a 
single intravenous push on 3 consecutive 
days. Cyclophosphamide was administered as 
a parenteral infusion over an hour period for 
one day and vincristine as an intravenous 
push on 2 consecutive days. 
Pathologic and Clinical Data 
Complete autopsies were performed on 33 
patients on these protocols. To minimize auto- 
lytic changes, all segments of G-I tract were 
opened, washed and fixed in 10% neutral buf- 
fered formalin immediately after evisceration. 
An average of 15 blocks was taken from the 
entire gastrointestinal tract and slides were 
stained with hematoxylin and eosin. Selected 
slides also were stained with alcian blue, 
mucicarmine, gram and silver methenamine 
stains. 
Charts were reviewed in all patients. Special 
attention was directed towards gastrointes- 
tinal symptomatology. Serum electrolytes and 
albumin values, records of body weight,

CYTOSINE ARABINOSIDE INDUCED GASTROINTESTINAL
TOXIC ALTERATIONS IN SEQUENTIAL
, A  Clinical-Pathologic Study of 33 Patients
RICHARD E. SLAVIN, MD,*$ MARCO A. DIAS, MD,§ AND REIN SARAL, MDt
Sequential chemotherapeutic regimens, primarily used in the treatment of
hematopoietic malignancies, and employing ara-C as a basic antineoplastic
agent induce mucosal alterations in the entire gastrointestinal tract. These
are characterized by surface and glandular epithelial atypia, immaturity, and
necrosis. Glandular regeneration is characteristically delayed leading to a state
of intestinal aproliferative cytopenia. Other toxic intestinal changes include
telangiectasia of blood vessels and the formation of intramural hematomas.
Intestinal infections develop frequently and are complicated by peritonitis,
liver abscesses, pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis and sepsis. These intestinal
lesions are accompanied by a predictable clinical syndrome which begins con-
comitantly with ara-C infusions and is characterized by diarrhea, ileus,
abdominal pain, hematemesis and melena, severe hypokalemia, hypocalcemia
and a protein-losing enteropathy. Additional toxic manifestations induced by
ara-C include transient weight gains, fever elevations and severe bone marrow
depression. The genesis of the intestinal lesions is linked to the three day
dose schedule of ara-C infusions which insures both arrest of the cycling
intestinal cells in the S-phase and a high cytotoxic index. The severity of
these lesions is markedly augmented by prior treatment with ara-C and
cyclophosphamide which causes synchronization and probable recruitment of
intestinal stem cells, respectively.

coma cell leukemia. Three patients on the
CAO protocol received 60 mg/mz of predni-
sone a day for 21 days and 0.025 mg/kg of

Complete autopsies were performed on 33
patients on these protocols. To minimize auto-
lytic changes, all segments of G-I tract were
opened, washed and fixed in 10% neutral buf-
fered formalin immediately after evisceration.
An average of 15 blocks was taken from the
entire gastrointestinal tract and slides were
stained with hematoxylin and eosin. Selected
slides also were stained with alcian blue,
mucicarmine, gram and silver methenamine
Charts were reviewed in all patients. Special
attention was directed towards gastrointes-
tinal symptomatology. Serum electrolytes and
albumin values, records of body weight,
temperature charts, and peripheral blood
A predictable clinical syndrome, which was
attributable to the modality of treatment as
distinct from the primary disease, developed
in most of the patients in this report. The
manifestations of this syndrome, which began
on days 8 to 10 of the Ac-D-Ac protocols and
on days 9 to 11 of the CY-ARA-C and CAO
regimens included gastrointestinal symptoms,
electrolyte imbalance and significant reduc-
tions in serum albumin. Fever, transient
weight gains, and alterations in renal function
Gastrointestinal Manifestations
These included diarrhea, ileus and/or ab-
dominal distention, abdominal pain of vary-
ing localizations, melena and upper gastroin-
testinal bleeding. The number of patients on
the different protocols exhibiting these symp-
Diarrhea was defined as the excretion of
two or more liters of stool per day and was
either intermittent or continuous and ranged
in duration from one to 19 days. Some pa-
tients died with diarrhea still in progress
and in several patients the diarrhea was
intractable to all medications.
Abdominal pain occurred in all quadrants
although it was our impression that the pain
was more prevalent in the right lower
Clinical ileus was confirmed by abdominal
flat plates which showed large bowel dila-
tation. Severe ileus and rebound tender-
ness in one patient prompted abdominal
Upper gastrointestinal bleeding generally
was controllable with blood transfusions.
However, in one patient it was massive and
led to a fatal surgical exploration.
Electrolyte Imbalance and Albumin Loss
Serum potassium levels decreased in almost
all patients following ara-C administration
(Fig. 1) and values remained below normal for
as long as two weeks even in the face of
parenteral potassium supplements. Studies of
stool electrolytes in some patients indicated
significant potassium loss in the feces. Thus,
losses of 40 to 60 mEq/liter were documented
in association with diarrhea of 8 to 10 liters
per day. Subsequent elevations of serum po-
tassium did occur in some patients and in-
variably were associated with the development
A significant drop in serum albumin con-
sistently was observed after treatment with
ara-C (Fig. 2). Some patients on the CAO and
CY-ara-C protocols also showed a moderate
drop in serum albumin after cyclophos-
phamide administration; this drop was ac-
centuated by the subsequent administration
of ara-C. The hypoalbuminemia also was pro-
longed and persisted despite parenteral ad-
A striking decrease in serum calcium was
noted in patients following ara-C infusions.
The drop in calcium values generally paral-
lelled the degree of albumin depletion (Fig. 2).
The criteria for diagnosing renal failure
in patients in this series were the elevation
of serum creatinine to twice normal and/or a
fall in creatinine clearance to less than 50%
of baseline. Impaired renal function de-
veloped in 85% of patients on the Ac-D-Ac
protocols and in 55% of patients on the other

The granulocyte counts fell dramatically
after ara-C infusions, dropping to below 1000/
mm3 with the lowest values ranging from 0
to 250/mm3. These counts generally persisted
for 2% to 3 weeks. Similarly, platelet counts

of the cell body. In many glands, mucus plugs
were evident in glandular lumens (Fig. 3). De-

nuclei and granular eosinophilic cytoplasm

Stage of Progressive Injury: Overall cellular
necrosis increased resulting in epithelial de-

glands showed cystic dilatation and the re-

Regeneration: Evidence of repair
was indicated by the resumption of mitotic
activity and the appearance of mature goblet
cells in the base of the crypts. Regeneration
began in the deep portions of the crypts
and appeared to extend upward (Fig. 6).
Initially, signs of regeneration were focal (Fig.
7), but in later stages the process of repair
was more extensive to include most of the
glands. Continuing necrosis of some glandular
cells was seen in some regenerating glands.
Some degree of superimposition of the
changes found in the three stages was ob-
served in most patients. This was most obvious
in patients placed on the Ac-D-Ac protocols
dying during or immediately after the second
Stromal Changes: Stromal changes were simi-
lar in the 3 stages. Total granulocytic de-
pletion from the lamina propria was noted
in all cases but plasma cell population
usually remained intact. Lymphocytes also
were reduced significantly in those patients
In essence the alterations observed in the
small bowel were similar to those seen in the
colonic mucosa. Necrosis and absence of cel-
lular renewal produced focal loss or shorten-
ing of villi (Fig. 8) and destruction of the crypts
of some glands. Paneth cell loss and recovery
In the gastric mucosa, similar epithelial
alterations were observed in the neck and
foveolar epithelium. The gastric glandular
compartment was generally spared except in
the antrum where the alterations also involved
FIG. 6. Stage of early regenera-
protocol day 19. Early regenera-

several colonic crypts. The higher
epithelial cells in these glands
are atypical, elongated and flat-
dilated and filled with mucus and
necrotic desquamated cells. The

atypical and nonmucus contain-
Pathologic changes involving the mucosal
epithelia consisted of atypia and focal epithe-
lial cell necrosis. These changes were most
pronounced in areas adjacent to mucosal ero-
sions or ulcers, i.e., areas of regeneration.
Chronology of Mucosal Alterations
The stage of initial injury was observed
during the first three days of ara-C infusions.
Progressive injury began thereafter and per-
sisted for as long as one week. Evidence of
early repair appeared as early as 5 days
after discontinuance of ara-C infusions in pa-
tients on the CAO and CY-ara-C protocols;
in patients on Ac-D-Ac protocols early repair
did not begin until 7 to 9 days after
termination of the second course of ara-C.
Generally, mucosal integrity was restored
within two weeks; however, foci of glandular
depopulation and atypical cells in scattered
glands still were seen as long as 28 days after
termination of ara-c protocols.
Gastrointestinal Vascular Changes
Telangiectasia of small blood vessels and
the formation of intramural hematomas were
present in the mucosa and submucosa.
Capillary telangiectasia in the submucosal
papillae of the esophagus was most prevalent
(Fig. 9). These vessels ruptured leading to
mucosal hemorrhages with denudation and
upper gastrointestinal bleeding. Similarly,

Intramural hematomas of varying numbers
and sizes were encountered in the esophagus
and small and large intestines in approxi-
mately one-third of our patients. These de-
veloped primarily in the submucosa; some ex-
tended into the mucosa and/or through the
muscularis causing either intestinal luminal
Distribution and Incidence: This complication
occurred in the entire gastrointestinal tract.

Colonic infections were found in 54.5% of
patients in this study. Our incidence of this
complication was 69% in the AC-D-AC pa-
tients and 4.5% in those on the CAO CY-
ARA-C protocols. The cecum was the pre-
dominant site of the infection and frequently
the infectious process backwashed into the
terminal ileum through the ileocecal valve.
Multiple ulcers occurred in approximately
Small intestinal infections were observed in
33% of the patients. The percentage of pa-
tients developing this complication was almost
identical in the CAO CY-ARA-C protocol
(35%) and the AC-D-AC regimens (31%).
There was no predilecting site of involvement
and in more than 50%, the infections
Esophageal ulcers were found in 54.5% of
patients. As in the colon, 69% of the pa-
tients on AC-D-AC protocols and 45% of
patients on the CAO CY-ARA-C showed
Gastric ulcers and erosions were found in
Patholopc Aspects of Infection: Grossly the
lesions appeared as irregular areas of mucosal
necrosis or as punched-out ulcers (Fig. 10).
Multiple lesions of this sort imparted a
(HD), day 15. Telangiectasia of

leopard-skin appearance to the involved por-
tions of the intestines. Vascular thrombosis
and infarction in the mucosa developed in
the vicinity of the infected glands and this
led to the production of intestinal ulcerations.
Causative Agents: Gram negative bacilli or
enterococci, alone or in combination with
Candida, accounted for most of the large in-
testinal lesions. Only one patient showed a
In contrast to the colon infections, Candida
alone caused almost one-half of the lesions in
the small intestine. Gram negative bacteria
alone were responsible for all the other ulcers
except in one patient who had a mixed bac-
Forty-four percent of the esophageal ulcers
were due to fungi, predominantly Candida.
Other causative organisms alone or in com-
bination included Herpes virus and Gram
negative bacteria. Etiologic organisms were
not identified in 22% of the patients.
In six patients, the gastric ulcers and ero-
sions were caused by Candida, and in one
case, the agent was Aspergillus; one patient
exhibited a diffuse necrotizing gastritis sec-
ondary to a mixed Candida and Pseudomonas
Pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis involv-
ing the bowel, stomach and esophagus was
seen as a complication of these ulcers in four
patients. A more serious complication of
these intestinal infections was the develop-
ment of metastatic liver abscesses which oc-
Kidneys: The kidneys of the patients dying in
renal failure showed marked interstitial
edema and dilatation of tubules, with flatten-
ing, focal atypia and occasional mitotic figures
Bone Marrow: The bone marrow in all pa-
tients was either hypocellular or aplastic.
Three patients on CAO and CY-ARA-C pro-
FIG. 10. Colon, Ac-D-Ac, day 19. Gram negative bac-
terial ulcers and mucosal necrosis. Multiple lesions impart
a characteristic “leopard skin” appearance to the colon.
tocols and four patients on Ac-D-Ac protocols
showed residual microfoci of leukemia.
Spleen and Lymph Nodes: Those patients on
cyclophosphamide containing protocols
showed moderate to severe lymphoid deple-
tion of the splenic white pulp and lymph
nodes. The patients on Ac-D-Ac protocols, on
the other hand, generally exhibited only mild
to moderate atrophy of the lymphoid
The results of this study show that chemo-
therapeutic protocols including ara-C, cyclo-
phosphamide, daunorubicin and vincristine
induce severe intestinal mucosal alterations
which are accompanied by a characteristic
clinical syndrome and intestinal infectious
complications. As previously reported by one
of us,21 high-dose intermittent cyclophos-
phamide does not cause comparable intesti-
nal lesions in man. From the review of the
literature and our personal observations in
autopsied material, treatment with dauno-
rubicin as a sole chemotherapeutic agent does
not induce gastrointestinal changes such as
described here. Vincristine may produce in-
testinal alterations, but only at dosages far
greater than those given to our patients.6 In
experimental studies single doses of ara-C
cause transient crypt epithelial damage.10,11,12
Moreover, ara-C given intraperitoneally every
three hours in doses of 15 mg/kg for 3 to 5
days has been shown to produce in the rodent,
lesions in the small intestine similar to those
seen in our patients.’O These observations
strongly suggest that ara-C, the one chemo-
therapeutic agent shared by all the regimens
discussed in this report, is the drug pri-
marily responsible for the intestinal lesions
and that the development of such lesions is
probably dose schedule dependent.
The genesis of the gastrointestinal mucosal
lesions stemmed from ara-C induced pertuba-
tions in the cell cycle of the rapidly pro-
liferating gastrointestinal epithelial  cell^.^^^^^
The employed regimens of ara-C, which were
designed to encompass the leukemic cell
cycle,8 not only blocked DNA ~ynthesis,’,’~
also were highly cytotoxic to many gastroin-
testinal stem cells. The progeny of those stem
cells escaping the S-phase arrest presumably
gave rise to the atypical epithelia lining the
glands and surface of the mucosa. The loss of
maturation capabilities in these latter cells may
reflect an ara-C induced inhibition of protein
synthesis15 and their continued death after
the cessation of ara-C regimens may be due to
ara-C induced chromosomal damage.15
The unexpected occurrence of a total
“freeze” in cellular division and subsequent
maturation of approximately one weeks dura-
tion after termination of the ara-C infusions
was a very important component of the ara-C
intestinal mucosal lesions. It led to an aprolif-
erative cytopenic state in the gut and was a
major cause of the prolongation of clinical
symptoms and serum abnormalities observed
in many of our patients. Moreover, this cell
cycle arrest was responsible for an intensifica-
tion of the mucosal lesions in the sequentially
timed Ac-D-Ac regimens since it assured
synchronization of intestinal stem cells at the
time that they were re-exposed to ara-C. Pos-
sibly this delay in cellular renewal was caused
by an ara-C induced inhibition of RNA and
protein synthesis during G,; such preparatory
events are deemed necessary for successful
progression through S-phase and subsequent
mitosis.2 Prior treatment with cyclophos-
phamide also may have augmented the
severity of the gut mucosal lesions since this
drug has been shown to recruit intestinal
Cellular degradative changes were char-
acterized by a granular type of necrosis and
the appearance of large vacuoles having a
polymorphic content. The latter structures,
termed karyolytic4j5 or apoptotic bodies,17
have been described as part of the normal
involutionary process in the intestines, i.e., an
intrinsically programmed “self-destruct mech-
anism”.17 They also have been observed, with
much greater frequency, following x-irradia-
and treatment with chemotherapeutic
agent^.'^,^^ Electron microscopic studies have
demonstrated that they represent large mem-
brane-bound vacuoles or phagolys~somes.~*-
5,17 Drugs which interfere with protein syn-
thesis12 and the administration of protein-free
diets5 have been shown to inhibit karyo-
lytic body formation in experimental animals.
A comparable situation may have occurred in
our patients who, while infused with ara-C, re-
ceived no protein supplements and were in a
negative protein balance due to loss of al-
bumin. Yet necrosis with formation of karyo-
lytic bodies was frequent. Whether this
would have been accentuated in the face of
adequate protein intake is at present a moot
The increased loss of protein in feces,
demonstrated prospectively in patients on our
protocols (unpublished data) and associated
with little or no protein ingestion and a
significant fall in serum albumin, suggests
that a protein-losing enteropathy existed in
almost all our patients. Its exact genesis
has not been clarified but the denuded sur-
face epithelia, immaturity of gut epithelia,
inadequate dietary protein and possible de-
pression of protein synthesis caused by the
chemotherapy all may play an etiologic role.
The fall in the serum calcium, as seen in
these patients, is related to the drop in serum
albumin since approximately half of the
serum calcium is bound to albumin.
Presumably the severe protracted hypo-
kalemia observed in our patients was due in
part to both mucus loss and diarrhea. Ab-
dominal distention and ileus may have been
caused by a hypokalemic induced intestinal
smooth muscle dysfunction and/or the de-
velopment of peritonitis secondary to the
extension of the intestinal infection to the
Telangiectasis of the type seen in the gastro-
intestinal tract of our patients has been de-
scribed following high-dose cyclophospha-
mide therapy.’l Such vascular changes have
been attributed to a direct toxic effect of the
metabolities of this agent. Since these changes
also were observed in patients treated with
ara-C without combination with cyclophos-
phamide, a direct non-specific toxic effect on
blood vessels also may occur with the high-
dose continuous regimens of ara-C as used in
The breakdown of the mucosal barrier
through loss of mucus production and sur-
face denudation combined with the destruc-
tion of granulocytes in the lamina propria and
bone marrow were the principal factors re-
sponsible for the serious intestinal infections
observed in our patients. Such infections were
caused primarily by an endogenous bacterial
The development of renal failure was multi-
factorial. Nephrotoxic antibiotics were used in
all patients, but we could not implicate a
specific antibiotic which could be held respon-
The reasons for the transient weight gains
seen in these patients are not known. Hypo-
albuminemia, fluid overload and possible
transient vascular damage with passage of
fluid in the extracellular spaces may play a
role. Unlike cyclophosphamide, ara-C in-
fusions are not associated with an inappropri-
ate secretion of antidiuretic h ~ r m o n e . ~
The fever elevations seen in our patients
were either drug-induced or related to the in-
fectious complications. Significantly, they
prompted additional antibiotic and fungal
therapy which in some patients contributed
to the development of renal failure.
Despite the severe intestinal changes de-
scribed in this report, a total of 46% of
patients on the CAO and 50% of patients on
high-dose Ac-D-Ac protocols survived to have
lengthy and complete remissions which did
not require maintenance chemotherapy.’
Through a better understanding of the patho-
physiology of the gastrointestinal complica-
tions gained in this study, we have prospec-
tively been able to reduce further the mortality
in the induction phase of acute leukemia
therapy using these protocols. This has been
accomplished by improving support facilities
and by anticipating electrolyte, protein and
Finally, it should be stressed that the
pathologic alterations described above are
part of a “new pathology” initiated by recent
innovative approaches in the treatment of
malignancy. A plea is made for pathologists
to conduct careful autopsy examinations in
these types of patients, for it is only through
such studies that unsuspected toxicities and
their complications may be uncovered, ap-
propriate management instituted, regimens
modified and, ultimately, patient survival
